text.title: Shadow Rollout Pattern — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every AI shadow rollout must satisfy. Skipping any one converts "shadow rollout" into "uncontrolled A/B" and the team learns about quality regressions from users.

rule.statement: The first stage is 100% shadow mirror (NOT a 1% real-traffic split). Only after the shadow window passes the gate does any real traffic see the candidate.

rationale.source: Anthropic AI deployment guidance; LaunchDarkly engineering blog

rule.rationale: A 1% real-traffic split exposes some users to a possibly-worse experience before the team has joint-metric data. Shadow first measures quality safely; real traffic comes after.

rule.statement: Promotion gate requires ALL three to be within contract for the shadow window: per-request quality delta ≥ -0.02 (or contract-specific), latency delta ≤ +20%, cost delta ≤ +10%. Any one out of band blocks promotion.

rationale.source: multi-objective optimisation; field experience with single-metric promotions

rule.rationale: A model that is +0.05 quality but +200% cost is not a win. A model that is +0.10 quality but 3x latency may degrade user experience more than the quality gain helps. The joint gate forces the trade-off to be explicit.

rule.statement: The judge model version and prompt are PINNED for the entire shadow window. Judge upgrades mid-window invalidate the comparison. Calibration audit (≥50 expert-labelled samples) MUST be ≤30 days old.

rationale.source: LLM-as-judge research (Zheng et al., 2023); production calibration practice

rule.rationale: Judge drift is the silent invalidator of shadow conclusions. A judge upgraded mid-window may rate the candidate higher because of the upgrade, not because the candidate is better. Calibration audit aligns the judge with expert ground truth.

rule.statement: Shadow window minimum: 48 hours AND ≥500 scored requests AND coverage of weekday + weekend patterns. Below any of these, the gate refuses to evaluate.
rule.rationale: A 6-hour window during work hours misses the consumer-shaped weekend traffic. 500 requests is the floor below which judge-score deltas have insufficient statistical power. Coverage prevents promoting on a non-representative sample.

rule.statement: After promotion, the shadow does NOT shut down. The previous version becomes the shadow at sampled traffic (≥5%) for ≥7 days, producing a continuous quality monitor that catches surprises in the new normal.

rationale.source: canary post-promotion monitoring; safety-critical deployment patterns

rule.rationale: Post-promotion is when novel real-world inputs hit the new model. The continuous shadow at sample rate is cheap insurance — if quality delta regresses, the team has fresh signal to roll back.

example.title: Shadow → promote → continuous shadow
example.code: Day 0: shadow at 100% mirror. 2.1k requests scored. Day 2: gate evaluation. Quality +0.04 (good), latency +8% (within), cost -3% (good). All three within contract. Coverage: weekday + weekend. Decision: promote. Day 3: 25% real traffic on candidate. Day 5: 100%. Old version moves to 5% shadow. Day 10: continuous shadow shows quality +0.04 stable. Decommission old version.
example.note: Illustrates r1 (shadow first), r2 (joint gate), r3 (judge pinned), r4 (window), r5 (continuous shadow).

example.title: The 1% rollout that surfaced as CSAT drop
example.code: Day 0: 1% real traffic on candidate. No error spike. Promote to 10%. Day 3: 25%. Day 5: 50%. Day 7: 100%. Day 14: CSAT down 4 points. Investigation: candidate is more concise but omits a section users relied on; no shadow judge ever scored it. Day 16: rollback. Two weeks of degraded experience for half the user base.
example.note: r1 (no shadow first), r2 (no joint quality gate). The rollout used "no errors" as the gate; the regression was in quality, not errors.
